metadata.title: The 510(k) Program: Evaluating Substantial Equivalence in Premarket Notifications [510(k)]
metadata.document_version: Final Guidance (supersedes K86-3 Guidance dated June 30, 1986)
metadata.note: Actual PDF content is the 510(k) Substantial Equivalence Premarket Notifications guidance for medical devices (CDRH/CBER), not a biosimilarity quality guidance. Rules extracted reflect the actual document content.

rules.rule_text: A manufacturer who intends to market a Class I, II, or III device intended for human use must submit to FDA a premarket notification submission (510(k)) at least 90 days before introducing the device into interstate commerce, unless exempt.
rules.source_section: Section IV, The 510(k) Decision-Making Process
rules.verbatim_quote: a manufacturer must submit to FDA a premarket notification submission (often referred to as a 510(k)), unless the device is exempt from the 510(k) requirements of the FD&C Act and does not exceed the limitations of exemptions for each of the device classification regulations... a manufacturer must submit a 510(k) to FDA at least 90 days before introducing, or delivering for introduction, a device into interstate commerce
rules.exceptions: Devices exempt from 510(k) requirements under applicable device classification regulations (21 CFR Parts 862-892, Section .9)

rules.rule_text: A new device cannot be commercialized until FDA issues an order (510(k) clearance) stating that the device has been determined to be substantially equivalent.
rules.source_section: Section IV, The 510(k) Decision-Making Process
rules.verbatim_quote: The device cannot be commercialized until FDA issues an order (510(k) clearance) stating that the device has been determined to be SE (Section 513(f)(1) of the FD&C Act (21 U.S.C. § 360c(f)(1))).

rules.rule_text: For substantial equivalence, the new device must have the same intended use as the predicate device AND the same technological characteristics, or different technological characteristics that do not raise different questions of safety and effectiveness and that demonstrate the device is at least as safe and effective as the predicate.
rules.source_section: Section IV.A, The 510(k) Review Standard
rules.verbatim_quote: the term 'substantially equivalent' or 'substantial equivalence' means, with respect to a device being compared to a predicate device, that the device has the same intended use as the predicate device and that the Secretary by order has found that the device (i) has the same technological characteristics as the predicate device, or (ii)(I) has different technological characteristics and the information submitted that the device is substantially equivalent to the predicate device contains information, including appropriate clinical or scientific data if deemed necessary by the Secretary...that demonstrates that the device is as safe and effective as a legally marketed device, and (II) does not raise different questions of safety and effectiveness than the predicate device.

rules.rule_text: A manufacturer must compare their new device to a similar legally marketed device (predicate) to support its substantial equivalence claim.
rules.source_section: Section IV, The 510(k) Decision-Making Process
rules.verbatim_quote: Manufacturers must compare their new device to a similar legally marketed device to support its substantial equivalence (21 U.S.C. § 360c(i); 21 CFR 807.92(a)(3)).

rules.rule_text: The use of a 'split predicate' is inconsistent with the 510(k) regulatory standard; a new device must have the same intended use as one predicate device and technological characteristics compared to that same predicate.
rules.source_section: Section IV.C, Predicate Device(s)
rules.verbatim_quote: the use of a 'split predicate' is inconsistent with the 510(k) regulatory standard. 'Split predicate' refers to a situation in which a manufacturer is attempting to 'split' the 510(k) decision making process by demonstrating that a new device has the same intended use as one marketed device while comparing the new device's technological characteristics with a second marketed device that has a different intended use.

rules.rule_text: FDA may only determine that a device is substantially equivalent to a predicate device if it has the same intended use as the predicate; a new intended use results in a finding of Not Substantially Equivalent.
rules.source_section: Section IV.D, Intended Use
rules.verbatim_quote: Under section 513(i) of the FD&C Act, FDA may only determine that a device is substantially equivalent to a predicate device if it has the same intended use.

rules.rule_text: The intended use of a device shall be determined based upon the proposed labeling submitted in a 510(k) submission.
rules.source_section: Section IV.D.2, Determining Intended Use
rules.verbatim_quote: Section 513(i)(1)(E)(i) of the FD&C Act provides that the FDA's determination of intended use of a device 'shall be based upon the proposed labeling' submitted in a 510(k).
rules.exceptions: FDA may also consider information outside the labeling under section 513(i)(l)(E) when there is reasonable likelihood the device will be used for an unidentified intended use that could cause harm

rules.rule_text: When a manufacturer identifies multiple predicate devices, a primary predicate must be identified in the submission to facilitate review.
rules.source_section: Section IV.C, Predicate Device(s)
rules.verbatim_quote: When a manufacturer does identify multiple predicates, the primary predicate refers to the one with indications for use and technological characteristics most similar to the device under review. Although using a single predicate is optimal, when multiple predicates are appropriate...FDA recommends identifying a primary predicate in the submission to facilitate a timely, well-supported decision.

rules.rule_text: The predicate device relied upon for substantial equivalence must be accurately cited in the 510(k) Summary according to 21 CFR 807.92(a)(3).
rules.source_section: Section IV.C.4, Identification and Documentation of the Predicate(s)
rules.verbatim_quote: Predicate device(s) relied upon for SE must be accurately cited in the 510(k) Summary (see Appendix B) according to 21 CFR 807.92 (a)(3).

rules.rule_text: Changes in indications for use that raise different questions of safety and effectiveness and preclude meaningful comparison with the predicate constitute a new intended use requiring PMA or De Novo.
rules.source_section: Section IV.D.3, Determining When Indications for Use Result in a New Intended Use
rules.verbatim_quote: Only a change in the indications for use that raises different questions of safety and effectiveness and therefore, precludes a meaningful comparison with the predicate device constitutes a new intended use.

rules.rule_text: The device description in a 510(k) must include information sufficient to explain the new device's technological characteristics, including similarities in materials, design, energy source, and other device features.
rules.source_section: Section IV.E.1, Identification of Technological Characteristics
rules.verbatim_quote: the device description in a 510(k) should include the information necessary to explain the new device's technological characteristics, including similarities in materials, design, energy source, and other device features. This information will be evaluated by FDA to determine whether the technological characteristics of the new device are different and, if so, whether they raise different questions of safety and effectiveness as compared to the predicate(s).

rules.rule_text: A 510(k) submission must also contain information about the technological characteristics of the predicate device to enable FDA to conduct a comparative assessment.
rules.source_section: Section IV.E.1, Step 1
rules.verbatim_quote: A 510(k) submission must also contain information about the technological characteristics of the predicate device (21 CFR 807.87(f), 807.92(a)(3) and (a)(6)). The manufacturer of the new device should provide information necessary and sufficient to fully and clearly identify and describe the technological characteristics of the predicate device so that FDA can conduct a comparative assessment of the technological characteristics.

rules.rule_text: FDA highly recommends that the manufacturer summarize technological characteristic comparisons in tabular format to facilitate review.
rules.source_section: Section IV.E.2, Step 2
rules.verbatim_quote: FDA highly recommends that the manufacturer summarize this information in tabular format to facilitate this step of review.

rules.rule_text: If technological characteristics of the new device raise different questions of safety and effectiveness compared to the predicate, the device is found Not Substantially Equivalent and must seek PMA approval or De Novo classification.
rules.source_section: Section IV.E.3, Step 3
rules.verbatim_quote: if the answer to Decision Point 4 is 'Yes' and the differences between the new device and predicate device raise different questions of safety and effectiveness, then the new device will be found NSE. Upon receipt of this type of NSE letter, the manufacturer may submit a PMA (or alternative submission type), or if appropriate, a De Novo request.

rules.rule_text: Performance data are typically needed in a Traditional 510(k) to demonstrate substantial equivalence; information on device performance described in labeling must be supported with appropriate performance data.
rules.source_section: Section IV.F, Requests for Performance Data
rules.verbatim_quote: performance data are typically needed in a Traditional 510(k) to demonstrate the substantial equivalence of a new device to a predicate device. In addition, information on device performance described in labeling or other sections of the 510(k) should be supported with appropriate performance data.
rules.exceptions: FDA may rely upon descriptive information alone to address Decision Points 1-4

rules.rule_text: Non-clinical laboratory studies that support the safety of medical devices must be conducted in compliance with 21 CFR Part 58, Good Laboratory Practice (GLP) for Nonclinical Laboratory Studies.
rules.source_section: Section IV.F, Requests for Performance Data
rules.verbatim_quote: Non-clinical laboratory studies that support the safety of medical devices must be conducted in compliance with 21 CFR Part 58, Good Laboratory Practice (GLP) for Nonclinical Laboratory Studies, as applicable, to ensure the quality, reliability, and integrity of study data.

rules.rule_text: Clinical data provided in support of any marketing application, including a 510(k), must constitute valid scientific evidence as defined in 21 CFR 860.7(c)(2) and must comply with Investigational Device Exemptions regulations.
rules.source_section: Section IV.F, Requests for Performance Data
rules.verbatim_quote: Clinical data provided in support of any marketing application, including a 510(k) when those data are relevant to a substantial equivalence determination, should constitute valid scientific evidence as defined in 21 CFR 860.7(c)(2) and must comply with the Investigational Device Exemptions (IDE) regulations as applicable.
rules.exceptions: Studies conducted outside the US generally do not have to comply with IDE regulations but should be conducted in accordance with good clinical practice

rules.rule_text: Whenever FDA requests information to demonstrate that devices with differing technological characteristics are substantially equivalent, FDA shall only request information necessary to making substantial equivalence determinations, consistent with the least burdensome principle.
rules.source_section: Section IV.A.2, The Least Burdensome Principle
rules.verbatim_quote: Whenever the Secretary requests information to demonstrate that devices with differing technological characteristics are substantially equivalent, the Secretary shall only request information that is necessary to making substantial equivalence determinations. In making such request, the Secretary shall consider the least burdensome means of demonstrating substantial equivalence and request information accordingly.
rules.exceptions: Nothing in this subparagraph shall alter the standard for determining substantial equivalence

rules.rule_text: If a manufacturer does not respond at all to FDA's requests for additional information, the submission will be subsequently withdrawn by FDA within the timeframe specified in FDA guidance.
rules.source_section: Section IV.A.3, Categories of NSE Determinations
rules.verbatim_quote: If the manufacturer does not respond at all to FDA's requests for additional information, the submission will be subsequently withdrawn by FDA within the timeframe specified by FDA's Guidance for Industry and FDA Staff, 'FDA and Industry Actions on Premarket Notification (510(k)) Submissions: Effect on FDA Review Clock and Goals'.

rules.rule_text: A new 510(k) that addresses deficiencies raised in an NSE letter must clearly identify how the outstanding issues have been addressed and cross-reference where the new information is provided; failure to cite the prior 510(k) number may result in a Refuse to Accept decision.
rules.source_section: Section IV.A.3, Categories of NSE Determinations
rules.verbatim_quote: the new 510(k) should clearly identify how the outstanding issues have been addressed and cross-reference where the new information is provided within the newly submitted 510(k). Failure to cite the prior 510(k) number may result in a Refuse to Accept decision.

rules.rule_text: The 510(k) Summary must include all elements identified in 21 CFR 807.92 and must be in sufficient detail to provide an understanding of the basis for a substantial equivalence determination.
rules.source_section: Section IV.G, The 510(k) Summary
rules.verbatim_quote: The 510(k) Summary is a document that provides a high-level discussion of the content of a 510(k) and must include all the elements identified in 21 CFR 807.92. A 510(k) Summary must be in sufficient detail to provide an understanding of the basis for a determination of substantial equivalence (21 CFR 807.92(a)).
rules.exceptions: A 510(k) Statement per 21 CFR 807.93 may be provided in lieu of a 510(k) Summary

rules.rule_text: The 510(k) Summary must include: submitter name and contact information; device name including trade name and classification name; identification of the legally marketed predicate device; description of the device; statement of intended use; and comparison of technological characteristics with the predicate.
rules.source_section: Appendix B, The 510(k) Summary Document Requirements
rules.verbatim_quote: 807.92(a)(1): 'The submitter's name, address, telephone number, a contact person, and the date the summary was prepared.' ...807.92(a)(2): 'The name of the device...' ...807.92(a)(3): 'An identification of the legally marketed device to which the submitter claims equivalence.' ...807.92(a)(4): 'A description of the device...' ...807.92(a)(5): 'A statement of the intended use of the device...' ...807.92(a)(6): [comparison of technological characteristics]

rules.rule_text: The 510(k) Summary must be in a separate section of the submission, beginning on a new page and ending on a page not shared with any other section, and should be clearly identified as a '510(k) summary'.
rules.source_section: Appendix B, The 510(k) Summary Document Requirements
rules.verbatim_quote: 807.92(c): 'The summary should be in a separate section of the submission, beginning on a new page and ending on a page not shared with any other section of the premarket notification submission, and should be clearly identified as a '510(k) summary'.'

rules.rule_text: A new device with a new design feature or added component must meet the SE standard with at least a single predicate from the same classification regulation.
rules.source_section: Section IV.C.1, Multiple Predicates
rules.verbatim_quote: A new device with a new design feature or added component must meet the SE standard with at least a single predicate from the same classification regulation.

rules.rule_text: A feature added to a new device that alters the intended use or introduces new or additional risk factors makes comparison to a predicate inadequate and renders the device ineligible for the 510(k) program.
rules.source_section: Section IV.C.1, Multiple Predicates
rules.verbatim_quote: If a feature is added which alters the intended use of the new device and/or alters the safety profile (i.e., introduces new or additional risk factors) such that comparison to a predicate cannot be made, the new device is ineligible for the 510(k) program.

rules.rule_text: For materials of construction, a complete identification of the detailed chemical formulation, especially for materials that come into contact with the patient, should be provided in the 510(k) device description.
rules.source_section: Section IV.E.1, Identification of Technological Characteristics
rules.verbatim_quote: For many devices, a complete identification of the detailed chemical formulation used in the materials of construction, especially for those materials that come into contact with the patient, should be provided. Note that the FDA does not clear/approve materials. Any additives, including color additives, coatings, or other surface modifications should also be identified.

rules.rule_text: Non-clinical animal and/or biocompatibility studies are typically requested when other forms of non-clinical bench performance testing are not sufficient to demonstrate substantial equivalence.
rules.source_section: Section IV.F, Requests for Performance Data
rules.verbatim_quote: Non-clinical animal and/or biocompatibility studies are typically requested when other forms of non-clinical bench performance testing are not sufficient to demonstrate substantial equivalence.

rules.rule_text: A finding of NSE based on a new intended use or technological characteristics that raise different safety/effectiveness questions classifies the device as Class III requiring PMA approval before marketing.
rules.source_section: Section IV.A.3, Categories of NSE Determinations
rules.verbatim_quote: The first category of NSE determinations includes a variety of different decisions, such as a finding of a lack of a predicate device, a new intended use, or different technological characteristics that raise different questions of safety or effectiveness when the new device is compared to the cited predicate device...the device is automatically classified into Class III and will require PMA approval, or if eligible, granting of a De Novo before marketing.
rules.exceptions: If FDA believes the device found NSE may be eligible for the De Novo program, the NSE letter will typically indicate FDA's recommendation

rules.rule_text: The indications for use statement in a 510(k) is also a factor in determining a device's intended use; consistency between the indications for use statement and the proposed labeling will facilitate the review.
rules.source_section: Section IV.D.1, Explanation of Intended Use and Indications for Use
rules.verbatim_quote: The indications for use statement in a 510(k) is also a factor in determining a device's intended use. Consistency between the indications for use statement and the proposed labeling will facilitate the review of the 510(k).

rules.rule_text: Changes in indications for use that may constitute a new intended use include: change from functional/performance to treatment or aesthetic indication; change from diagnostic to screening; change in anatomical structure; change in patient population; and change in clinical context or setting.
rules.source_section: Section IV.D.4, Changes in Indications for Use that May Result in a New Intended Use
rules.verbatim_quote: Certain types of changes...warrant particular attention in evaluating whether the new indications for use result in a new intended use because they are more likely to significantly affect safety or effectiveness: a change from a functional/performance indication to a treatment or aesthetic indication; a change from a diagnostic indication to a screening indication, or vice versa; a change in the anatomical structure of use; a change in the patient population (e.g., adult versus pediatric; different disease populations); a change in the clinical context or setting (e.g., periodic monitoring versus continuous monitoring; hospital versus home use).
